message.content:
An adversary can forge SAML tokens if they possess a valid SAML token-signing certificate, allowing them to authenticate across services using SAML 2.0 for single sign-on (SSO). The default token lifetime is one hour, but adversaries can manipulate the validity period. By compromising an organization's token-signing certificate or establishing a new federation trust with their own AD FS server, adversaries can create forged tokens. This technique differs from stealing tokens as the tokens are newly created by the adversary. Forged SAML tokens can grant administrative Azure AD privileges, potentially bypassing multi-factor authentication and other security measures. Tools like AADInternals can facilitate this process. Notably, APT29 has used compromised SAML signing certificates to create tokens.

Mitigation strategies include securing token-signing certificates, monitoring for unusual federation trust activities, and implementing robust logging and alerting mechanisms to detect anomalies in SAML token usage. Regularly rotating certificates and enforcing strict access controls can also help mitigate this threat.
